    xpBonus:40, moralBonus:20 },
  { id:'radioddity_db25', brand:'Radioddity', name:'Radioddity DB25-D Dual Band HT',
    type:'HT', cost:28, rigBonus:1, antennaBonus:0, licReq:0,
    desc:'Radioddity DB25-D DMR/analog dual-band HT. VHF/UHF, 5W, Tier I/II DMR.',
    flavor:'Budget DMR in a small package. The DB25-D handles analog and Tier II digital.',
    note:'DB25-D competes with entry Hytera and TYT. Decent DMR for the price.',
    xpBonus:10, moralBonus:8 },
  { id:'baofeng_uv5r', brand:'Baofeng', name:'Baofeng UV-5R Dual Band HT',
    type:'HT', cost:15, rigBonus:1, antennaBonus:0, licReq:0,
    desc:'Baofeng UV-5R dual-band 5W HT. VHF/UHF FM. The worlds most popular ham radio.',
    flavor:'The UV-5R chirps. It receives everything. It transmits questionably on some things.',
    note:'Over 50 million UV-5Rs sold. The entry radio for millions of new hams worldwide.',
    xpBonus:8, moralBonus:8 },
  { id:'hamclock_inovato', brand:'Inovato', name:'HamClock by Inovato (7in Touch)',
    type:'Shack Accessory', cost:55, rigBonus:0, antennaBonus:0, licReq:0,
    desc:'HamClock on Inovato hardware with 7in touchscreen. Solar data, propagation, maps.',
    flavor:'The HamClock glows. Gray-line map, solar flux, band conditions — all at a glance.',
    note:'HamClock by WB0OEW on Inovato-Q40 hardware. Real-time propagation dashboard.',
    xpBonus:12, moralBonus:10 },

  // ══ CELLULAR / WIFI TOWER INFRASTRUCTURE ═════════════════════════════════
  { id:'tower_4g_lte', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'4G LTE Base Station Tower',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:150000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:10, licReq:3,
    desc:'Full 4G LTE macro base station. 60m lattice tower, 3-sector panel array, eNodeB cabinet, microwave backhaul dish, diesel generator backup, GPS timing unit. Licensed spectrum. 10 km coverage radius.',
    flavor:'The 4G tower powers up. 10 km radius of LTE coverage. Every device in range connects instantly.',
    note:'eNodeB 3-sector 20+20+20 MHz LTE. 2x2 MIMO. Ericsson RBS6000 or Nokia AirScale spec. Backhaul via licensed 23 GHz microwave. Requires Telco/carrier licence. Rigbonus 5, antenna +10.',
    xpBonus:500, moralBonus:200 },

  { id:'tower_5g_nr', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'5G NR mmWave/Sub-6 Tower',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:150000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:10, licReq:3,
    desc:'Dual-band 5G New Radio base station. 40m monopole with 64T64R massive MIMO array for Sub-6 GHz (n78 3.5 GHz) plus 32-beam mmWave panel (n261 28 GHz). gNodeB with fronthaul CPRI over fibre. Beamforming. NSA/SA capable.',
    flavor:'5G NR active. Massive MIMO locks 64 simultaneous beams. The mmWave panel lights up the intersection.',
    note:'64T64R Sub-6 + n261 mmWave. Ericsson AIR 6449 or Huawei AAU5619 spec equivalent. gNB cloud-native. O-RAN fronthaul. Requires mmWave + mid-band spectrum licence. Highest antenna bonus in game.',
    xpBonus:500, moralBonus:200 },

  // ══ LEGACY CELLULAR TOWER INFRASTRUCTURE ═════════════════════════════════
  { id:'tower_altai', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'Altai Super Base Station (ABS)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:8000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:5, licReq:2,
    desc:'Altai Technologies ABS-02S Super Base Station. Single-unit 802.11n WiFi base with 1-2 km urban range and up to 8 km rural. Roof-mount or tower-top. Backhaul via Ethernet. Self-contained unit — no separate antenna needed. Used in remote and developing-world deployments.',
    flavor:'The Altai ABS powers up on the rooftop. Eight kilometres of WiFi coverage from one box.',
    note:'Altai ABS covers entire villages with a single unit. 802.11n 2.4/5 GHz. Popular in Pacific Islands, Africa, remote Australia. No tower required — roof or pole mount suffices.',
    xpBonus:60, moralBonus:40 },

  { id:'tower_altai_c1', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'Altai C1n Super Base Station',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:10000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:6, licReq:2,
    desc:'Altai C1n upgrade. Dual-band 802.11n with enhanced smart antenna system. 4 km urban, 12 km rural LOS range. PoE powered. Manages 256 concurrent clients. 300 Mbps throughput. Weatherproof IP67.',
    flavor:'Twelve kilometres of 802.11n from a single IP67 unit on a 10m pole. The village is online.',
    note:'Altai C1n smart antenna suppresses interference. Ideal for rural broadband where fibre is unavailable. Popular with ISPs in Asia-Pacific.',
    xpBonus:75, moralBonus:45 },

  { id:'tower_1g_amps', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G AMPS Cell Tower (Advanced Mobile Phone System)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:5000, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:4, licReq:2,
    desc:'Original 1G AMPS analogue cellular tower. 800 MHz band, 30 kHz FM channels, FDMA. Omnidirectional fibreglass whip antennas on 40m guyed mast. MTSO connection via copper T1. Covers approx 25 km radius. Historic — AMPS decommissioned in USA 2008.',
    flavor:'The AMPS tower hisses with analogue FM. Every call unencrypted. You can hear the neighbours.',
    note:'AMPS 800 MHz analogue. No encryption whatsoever — any scanner can decode calls. Decommissioned US 2008, Canada 2007. Rare operating examples now in museums and ham radio collections.',
    xpBonus:45, moralBonus:35 },

  { id:'tower_1g_nmt', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G NMT-450/900 Tower (Nordic Mobile Telephone)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:5500, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:4, licReq:2,
    desc:'NMT Nordic Mobile Telephone 1G tower. 450 or 900 MHz analogue FDMA. Used across Scandinavia, Eastern Europe, Russia. Directional panel antennas on 35m lattice mast. NMT-450 range up to 70 km via high-gain panels.',
    flavor:'NMT-450. 70 km range from a single site. The Soviets loved this technology for rural coverage.',
    note:'NMT pioneered automatic roaming between countries in 1981 — first international cellular roaming. NMT-450 still has licensed operators in some former Soviet states.',
    xpBonus:42, moralBonus:32 },

  { id:'tower_1g_tacs', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G TACS Tower (Total Access Communication System)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:4800, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:3, licReq:2,
    desc:'TACS analogue 1G tower. UK/Europe variant of AMPS. 890-960 MHz, 25 kHz channels. Used in UK, Ireland, Italy, Spain, Austria. Panel antennas on 30m mast. BT Cellnet and Vodafone UK used TACS from 1985.',
    flavor:'TACS tower on a British hillside. You catch a fragment of an unencrypted analogue call. Classic.',
    note:'TACS decommissioned UK 2000. ETACS extended variant added 40 MHz of channels. Princess Diana conversations famously intercepted on TACS scanners in 1990s.',
    xpBonus:40, moralBonus:30 },

  { id:'tower_2g_gsm', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G GSM BTS Tower (900/1800 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:25000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:6, licReq:2,
    desc:'GSM 2G Base Transceiver Station. 900 or 1800 MHz, 200 kHz TDMA, 8 timeslots per carrier. Typical 2-4 TRX per sector, 3-sector site. 30m monopole or rooftop. Ericsson RBS 2000 or Nokia MetroSite spec. Still active globally for IoT and fallback voice.',
    flavor:'GSM BTS online. Eight timeslots per carrier. The neighbourhood pings the BCCH immediately.',
    note:'GSM BTS still operates in 180+ countries as of 2025. IoT SIM cards (M2M) rely on it. A5/1 encryption cracked — avoid for sensitive calls. Ericsson RBS2000 workhorses still running.',
    xpBonus:120, moralBonus:70 },

  { id:'tower_2g_cdma', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G CDMA IS-95 BTS Tower (850/1900 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:28000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:6, licReq:2,
    desc:'IS-95 cdmaOne 2G CDMA Base Station. 850 or 1900 MHz, 1.25 MHz spread spectrum, soft handoff. Qualcomm CDMA technology. Motorola SC series or Lucent CDMA2000 BTS. Used by early Verizon and Sprint. Capacity advantage over GSM in high-density areas.',
    flavor:'CDMA IS-95 pilot channel acquired. Soft handoff active on three sectors simultaneously.',
    note:'IS-95 CDMA uses spread spectrum — harder to intercept than GSM. Soft handoff gives better coverage at cell edges. Evolved to CDMA2000 EV-DO for data.',
    xpBonus:125, moralBonus:70 },

  { id:'tower_2g_gprs', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G GPRS/EDGE Data Tower Upgrade',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:15000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:5, licReq:2,
    desc:'GPRS/EDGE upgrade for existing GSM BTS. GPRS adds packet data to GSM (114 kbps). EDGE Enhanced Data Rates adds 8PSK modulation (384 kbps). PCU (Packet Control Unit) cabinet adds to existing BTS. Widely deployed 2001-2010.',
    flavor:'GPRS packet switched. 114 kbps on the best days. Enough for WAP pages and MMS.',
    note:'GPRS was 2.5G — first mobile internet. EDGE (2.75G) improved to 384 kbps. Both still used for IoT/M2M globally in 2025. Upgrade requires existing GSM BTS.',
    xpBonus:80, moralBonus:45 },

  { id:'tower_3g_wcdma', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G WCDMA/UMTS Node B Tower',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:55000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:3,
    desc:'3G UMTS Node B base station. 2100 MHz (EU) or 850/1900 MHz (US), 5 MHz WCDMA channels. RNC (Radio Network Controller) manages handoff. 3 sectors, 2x2 MIMO optional. Ericsson RBS3000/3900 or Nokia BTS3000 spec. 14.4 Mbps HSDPA. Used for voice VoIP fallback 2025.',
    flavor:'WCDMA Node B synced. HSDPA 14 Mbps active. The smartphone connects and loads a real webpage.',
    note:'WCDMA still mandatory for VoLTE fallback in many networks. IMS architecture for voice. Node B decommission ongoing but not complete globally. 2100 MHz band still licensed.',
    xpBonus:200, moralBonus:100 },

  { id:'tower_3g_cdma2000', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G CDMA2000 1x/EV-DO BTS Tower',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:58000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:3,
    desc:'CDMA2000 3G base station. 1xRTT for voice + EV-DO Rev A for data (3.1 Mbps down, 1.8 Mbps up). 850/1900 MHz 1.25 MHz spread spectrum. Motorola BSS or Nortel CDMA BTS. Sprint and Verizon primary 3G technology. Deployed 2000-2022.',
    flavor:'EV-DO Rev A handshake complete. 3.1 Mbps downlink. In 2003 this felt like the future.',
    note:'CDMA2000 EV-DO Rev A delivered best 3G data speeds in its era. Verizon shut down CDMA network 31 Dec 2022. Sprint (T-Mobile) shutdown 2022. Towers now repurposed for Band 71/n71.',
    xpBonus:210, moralBonus:100 },

  { id:'tower_3g_hsdpa', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G HSPA+/DC-HSPA Tower (21/42 Mbps)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:65000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:3,
    desc:'HSPA+ evolved 3G tower. Dual-Carrier DC-HSPA 42 Mbps downlink, 11 Mbps uplink. 2x2 MIMO. Called 3.75G or 4G by some operators (controversially). Ericsson RBS 6000 HSPA+ or Nokia Flexi BTS. Still used as 4G coverage gap filler in rural areas 2025.',
    flavor:'DC-HSPA 42 Mbps. Marketing calls it 4G. The antenna engineers call it 3G with lipstick. Works fine.',
    note:'HSPA+ 42 Mbps genuinely fast for its era. Many operators used it as interim before LTE rollout. Still active in rural areas globally where LTE deployment remains incomplete.',
    xpBonus:220, moralBonus:110 },

  // ══ AT&T MICROWAVE NETWORK & FULL GENERATION TOWER SET ═══════════════════
  { id:'tower_att_mw_orig', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'AT&T Long Lines Microwave Tower (Original)',
    type:'Microwave Tower', cost:12000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:6, licReq:2,
    desc:'Original AT&T Long Lines cross-country microwave relay tower. 1950s-1970s. 60-90m concrete or steel lattice structure. 4 GHz and 6 GHz horn-reflector antennas (the iconic bullet-shaped pods). Carried transcontinental telephone trunk traffic and early television before fibre. Spaced ~50 km apart across the USA.',
    flavor:'The horn-reflector pods stare across the plains. 4 GHz microwave, line of sight, 50 km to the next tower.',
    note:'AT&T Long Lines microwave network carried 99% of US long-distance traffic at its peak. Towers still standing across USA as landmarks. Horn-reflector antennas now iconic mid-century design objects.' },

  { id:'tower_att_mw_tn', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'AT&T TN-1 Microwave Relay (TD-2 System)',
    type:'Microwave Tower', cost:16000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:6, licReq:2,
    desc:'AT&T TD-2 coast-to-coast microwave backbone tower. TD-2 carried 2400 voice circuits per hop. 4 GHz band, FM modulation, shielded horn-reflector antennas in cylindrical radomes. AT&T built first TD-2 coast-to-coast route 1951. The backbone of Cold War-era US communications.',
    flavor:'TD-2 system: 2400 voice circuits in a 4 GHz beam. Cold War communications at its finest.',
    note:'TD-2 was first coast-to-coast microwave system 1951. Critical for Cold War continuity-of-government communications. NSA and AUTOVON used AT&T Long Lines as backbone.' },

  { id:'tower_att_mw_mod', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'AT&T Modern Licensed Microwave Link',
    type:'Microwave Tower', cost:35000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:2,
    desc:'Modern AT&T licensed point-to-point microwave backhaul. 6/11/18/23 GHz parabolic dish antennas on 40m steel monopole. 1 Gbps+ capacity. Licensed spectrum. Used for cellular backhaul where fibre unavailable. Adaptive modulation 4QAM to 4096QAM.',
    flavor:'Modern microwave backhaul. 1 Gbps in a pencil-thin 23 GHz beam across 15 km. Invisible and fast.',
    note:'Modern licensed P2P microwave carries most cellular backhaul where fibre is not deployed. AT&T uses Ericsson MINI-LINK and Nokia Wavence hardware. Adaptive QAM adjusts to weather.' },

  // ── 0G: PRE-CELLULAR MOBILE RADIO ─────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_0g_mrs', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'0G Mobile Radio System Tower (MRS)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:800, rigBonus:1, antennaBonus:2, licReq:1,
    desc:'0G Mobile Radio System. Pre-cellular manually-operated mobile telephone. VHF/UHF base station tower, operator connects calls manually. Push-to-talk. No handoff. Single base covers entire city. Motorola Improved Mobile Telephone Service (IMTS) 150/450 MHz. 1940s-1980s.',
    flavor:'The 0G base operator answers. You press PTT. Somewhere across town, someone receives. No handoff. Ever.',
    note:'0G IMTS: up to 23 channels per market. Maximum ~40 mobile subscribers per frequency. Waitlists of years in major cities. Manual operator connection. PTT half-duplex.' },

  { id:'tower_0g_imts', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'0G IMTS Tower (Improved Mobile Telephone)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:1200, rigBonus:1, antennaBonus:2, licReq:1,
    desc:'IMTS Improved Mobile Telephone Service tower. 150 MHz and 450 MHz. Automatic dialling, full duplex voice. Single high-power omni antenna covers 50+ km radius. Deployed by AT&T and Bell System 1969-1983. The system that made cellular necessary.',
    flavor:'IMTS: full duplex automatic mobile phone. One transmitter. The whole city. 40 subscribers maximum.',
    note:'IMTS solved PTT with full duplex but still no handoff or reuse. AT&T New York IMTS had 2000+ person waitlist. Scarcity drove the invention of cellular frequency reuse.' },

  { id:'tower_0g_autotel', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'0G Autotel / PALM Tower (Canada)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:1000, rigBonus:1, antennaBonus:2, licReq:1,
    desc:'Autotel/PALM (Public Automated Land Mobile) 0G tower. Canadian Bell system. 72 MHz band. Digital signalling with analog voice. Zoned service area. Used in Ontario and Quebec 1970s-1990s. Better spectrum efficiency than IMTS through digital control channels.',
    flavor:'Autotel: digital signalling, analogue voice. Canada solved mobile telephony quietly in 1972.',
    note:'Autotel used digital control before cellular existed. Semi-automatic handoff between zones. Phased out with cellular arrival but considered elegant for its era.' },

  // ── 1G: ANALOGUE CELLULAR ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_1g_amps_att', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G AT&T AMPS Tower (Bell System)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:4500, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:3, licReq:1,
    desc:'AT&T/Bell System AMPS 1G tower. The original cellular network. 800 MHz, 30 kHz FM channels, 666 channels per market split A/B. 40m monopole with fibreglass omni antennas. AT&T launched AMPS in Chicago 1983. Automatic handoff between cells — revolutionary.',
    flavor:'AMPS 1983: the first real cellular network. 666 channels. Handoff works. The modern world begins.',
    note:'AT&T AMPS launched 12 Oct 1983 Chicago. First operational cellular system in USA. 30 kHz FM voice, no encryption. Any scanner receives. Decommissioned Feb 2008.' },

  { id:'tower_1g_c_netz', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G C-Netz Tower (Germany 450 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:4200, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:3, licReq:1,
    desc:'German C-Netz 1G analogue cellular. 450 MHz, 20 kHz channels, 222 base stations covering all of West Germany. Deutsche Bundespost operated. First German cellular 1985. 800,000 subscribers at peak. Shutdown 2000. 450 MHz band later reused for CDMA450 and now LTE450.',
    flavor:'C-Netz 450 MHz. The whole of West Germany covered by 222 base stations. Ambitious 1985 engineering.',
    note:'C-Netz was world first to achieve national coverage. 450 MHz propagation meant fewer towers needed. Calls traceable by police without warrant — frequent concern in 1980s Germany.' },

  { id:'tower_1g_jtacs', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'1G J-TACS/NTT Tower (Japan 900 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:4000, rigBonus:2, antennaBonus:3, licReq:1,
    desc:'Japanese J-TACS and NTT 1G analogue tower. 900 MHz, 12.5 kHz channels. NTT launched Japan first cellular 1979 — before USA and Europe. J-TACS and JTACS derivatives operated by DDI Cellular and IDO. Dense urban micro-cell deployments.',
    flavor:'Japan had cellular in 1979. While others planned, NTT deployed. 12.5 kHz channels, dense urban grid.',
    note:'Japan NTT launched mobile phone service Dec 1979 — the worlds first commercial cellular network. Backpack-sized phones initially. Car phones standard. Rapid miniaturisation by 1987.' },

  // ── 2G: DIGITAL CELLULAR ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_2g_gsm_att', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G GSM AT&T Tower (850/1900 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:22000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:5, licReq:2,
    desc:'AT&T Mobility GSM 2G BTS. 850 and 1900 MHz dual-band. 200 kHz TDMA, 8 timeslots. A5/1 voice encryption. Ericsson RBS 2116/2216 or Nokia BTS-2000 hardware. AT&T GSM launched 1996 in USA. Still active for IoT/M2M and emergency fallback 2025.',
    flavor:'AT&T GSM BTS: dual-band 850/1900. Eight timeslots. The SIM card authenticates. The city connects.',
    note:'AT&T GSM 850 MHz launched 2001 alongside 1900 MHz. 850 MHz provides superior building penetration. A5/1 encryption cracked since 2009 by rainbow tables. IoT SIMs still rely on this network.' },

  { id:'tower_2g_damps', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G D-AMPS IS-136 Tower (AT&T)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:18000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:4, licReq:2,
    desc:'D-AMPS Digital AMPS IS-136 tower. AT&T transitional 2G. TDMA overlay on AMPS 800 MHz spectrum. 30 kHz channels, 3 users per channel via TDMA. Backward compatible with AMPS handsets. AT&T used IS-136 1995-2002 before migrating to GSM. Also called TDMA or Digital AMPS.',
    flavor:'D-AMPS IS-136: three digital calls where one analogue used to go. The spectrum efficiency doubles.',
    note:'IS-136 D-AMPS backward compatible with AMPS — phone would automatically fall back. AT&T retired IS-136 when migrating to GSM Cingular 2002-2004. Some rural towers remained until 2008.' },

  { id:'tower_2g_pdc', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G PDC Tower (Japan Personal Digital Cellular)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:20000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:5, licReq:2,
    desc:'Japanese PDC Personal Digital Cellular 2G tower. 800/1500 MHz, TDMA, 25 kHz channels. NTT DoCoMo standard. Launched 1993. 80 million subscribers at peak. Supported i-mode mobile internet 1999 — years before 3G. Shutdown completed 2012.',
    flavor:'PDC and i-mode: Japan had mobile internet in 1999. WAP was irrelevant here. DoCoMo led the world.',
    note:'PDC i-mode launched 1999 with 9.6 kbps internet. Millions used it for email and news before 3G. NTT DoCoMo i-mode proved mass-market mobile internet was viable — lessons ignored in the West.' },

  { id:'tower_2g_gsm1800', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'2G GSM-1800 DCS Tower (Europe)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:23000, rigBonus:3, antennaBonus:5, licReq:2,
    desc:'GSM-1800 DCS (Digital Cellular System) tower. 1800 MHz, 200 kHz TDMA. Smaller cells due to propagation. Used for capacity in urban areas alongside GSM-900 for coverage. All major European operators deployed DCS-1800 from 1993. T-Mobile and Orange original DCS-1800 operators in Germany/UK.',
    flavor:'DCS-1800: urban capacity layer over GSM-900. 1800 MHz fills the city centre. 900 MHz covers the hills.',
    note:'DCS-1800 enabled European cellular expansion beyond 900 MHz capacity limits. Dual-band 900/1800 phones became standard 1997. UK had 4 GSM networks — world first multi-operator GSM market.' },

  // ── 3G: BROADBAND MOBILE ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_3g_umts_att', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G UMTS AT&T Node B (2100/850 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:50000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:6, licReq:3,
    desc:'AT&T Mobility UMTS 3G Node B. 2100 MHz primary, 850 MHz coverage layer. 5 MHz WCDMA. HSDPA 14.4 Mbps, HSUPA 5.76 Mbps. Ericsson RBS 3418 or Nokia BTS3000. AT&T UMTS launched 2004. IMS core for VoIP. Nationwide coverage by 2007. HSPA+ evolved to 21/42 Mbps.',
    flavor:'AT&T UMTS Node B online. HSDPA 14.4 Mbps. The iPhone 3G finally has a network to run on.',
    note:'AT&T launched UMTS 2004 to support push-to-talk and smartphone data. iPhone 3G launch July 2008 caused immediate AT&T UMTS congestion. Network expansion 2008-2010 emergency program.' },

  { id:'tower_3g_td_scdma', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G TD-SCDMA Tower (China Mobile)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:45000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:6, licReq:3,
    desc:'TD-SCDMA Time-Division Synchronous CDMA 3G tower. Chinese indigenous 3G standard. TDD 2010-2015 MHz band. 5 MHz TDD channels. China Mobile TD-SCDMA network — 460,000 base stations at peak. One of three ITU 3G standards alongside WCDMA and CDMA2000.',
    flavor:'TD-SCDMA: Chinas own 3G. 460,000 base stations. The largest 3G network in the world.',
    note:'TD-SCDMA was Chinese-developed to avoid Qualcomm CDMA royalties. China Mobile operated largest TD-SCDMA network globally. Technology foundation for later TDD-LTE and TD-NR (5G).' },

  { id:'tower_3g_hspa_plus', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'3G HSPA+ DC-HSPA Tower (42 Mbps)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:62000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:3,
    desc:'HSPA+ Dual-Carrier evolved 3G. 42 Mbps downlink via DC-HSPA, 11 Mbps uplink. 2x2 MIMO optional on single carrier. AT&T marketed as 4G in 2010-2012 despite being 3G. Ericsson RBS 6000 HSPA+ upgrade. T-Mobile USA 42 Mbps HSPA+ covered 200M POPs by 2012.',
    flavor:'DC-HSPA 42 Mbps: AT&T calls it 4G. Engineers call it HSPA+. Users call it fast enough.',
    note:'T-Mobile USA and AT&T both marketed HSPA+ as 4G 2010-2012, confusing consumers. ITU clarified LTE Advanced and WiMAX Advanced are true 4G. HSPA+ remains in service globally 2025.' },

  // ── 4G: LTE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_4g_lte_att', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'4G LTE AT&T eNodeB Tower',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:140000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:8, licReq:3,
    desc:'AT&T 4G LTE eNodeB macro base station. Bands 2/4/5/12/14/17/29/30/66 (wide carrier aggregation). 20 MHz channels, 2x2 or 4x4 MIMO. Cat-18 CA peak 1.2 Gbps. Ericsson AIR 3239 or Nokia AirScale BTS. FirstNet Band 14 dedicated public safety LTE. AT&T LTE launched 2011 Dallas/Atlanta.',
    flavor:'AT&T LTE eNodeB: carrier aggregation across 5 bands. 1.2 Gbps peak. FirstNet Band 14 live.',
    note:'AT&T LTE first launched 2011. FirstNet public safety LTE on Band 14 700 MHz — dedicated spectrum for police/fire/EMS nationwide. AT&T 4G covers 99% of US population by 2025.' },

  { id:'tower_4g_lte_advanced', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'4G LTE-Advanced Tower (Cat-18 CA)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:148000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:9, licReq:3,
    desc:'LTE-Advanced Pro eNodeB. 5-carrier aggregation, 256QAM DL, 64QAM UL, 4x4 MIMO. Cat-18 UE peak 1.2 Gbps. Licensed and unlicensed spectrum aggregation (LAA). Massive MIMO option 32T32R. Ericsson AIR 6449 or Nokia AirScale AHB. True IMT-Advanced compliant.',
    flavor:'LTE-Advanced Pro: 5-carrier aggregation, 256QAM, 4x4 MIMO. 1.2 Gbps. This is proper 4G.',
    note:'LTE-A Pro (3GPP Release 13+) is genuine ITU IMT-Advanced 4G. Often deployed as software upgrade on existing LTE hardware. Required for VoLTE HD voice and low-latency gaming.' },

  { id:'tower_4g_volte', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'4G VoLTE IMS Core Upgrade',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:80000, rigBonus:4, antennaBonus:7, licReq:3,
    desc:'VoLTE (Voice over LTE) IMS core upgrade for existing LTE network. P-CSCF, I-CSCF, S-CSCF, HSS, PCRF, P-GW components. HD Voice (EVS/AMR-WB codec). HD Video calling. Dedicated QCI 1 bearer for voice. Required for 3G/2G switch-off.',
    flavor:'VoLTE IMS core live. HD Voice confirmed. Call quality jumps from AM radio to studio audio.',
    note:'VoLTE IMS core required before 3G switch-off. Without IMS, LTE devices fall back to 3G/2G for voice (CSFB). AT&T mandated VoLTE for all new devices 2017. HD Voice on VoLTE dramatically better than 3G AMR.' },

  // ── 5G: NEW RADIO ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  { id:'tower_5g_low_band', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'5G NR Low-Band Tower (600/700 MHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:130000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:8, licReq:3,
    desc:'5G NR low-band gNodeB. n71 (600 MHz) and n28 (700 MHz). 8T8R antenna array. Up to 100 MHz channel (n71). 400+ Mbps typical. Excellent coverage — 15 km+ radius rural. T-Mobile n71 nationwide 5G uses this band. Covers inside buildings and rural areas where mmWave cannot.',
    flavor:'Low-band 5G: 600 MHz. The signal goes through walls, through hills, 15 km to the next town.',
    note:'T-Mobile n71 600 MHz nationwide 5G covers more geography than all other 5G combined. Low-band speeds 50-400 Mbps but massive coverage. AT&T n14 FirstNet 700 MHz 5G for public safety.' },

  { id:'tower_5g_mid_band', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'5G NR Mid-Band Tower (2.5/3.5 GHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:145000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:9, licReq:3,
    desc:'5G NR mid-band gNodeB. n41 (2.5 GHz) and n78 (3.5 GHz). 32T32R or 64T64R massive MIMO. 100 MHz channels. 1-3 Gbps peak. The sweet-spot 5G band — coverage and capacity balanced. T-Mobile n41 extended range uses beamforming to cover 2-5 km cells.',
    flavor:'Mid-band 5G: 64T64R massive MIMO on 3.5 GHz. 64 simultaneous beams. Gigabit speeds across 3 km.',
    note:'Mid-band 5G (C-band / n78) is the primary 5G capacity band globally. AT&T and Verizon acquired C-band (n77/n78) at FCC auction for record $68 billion combined. Deployed 2022+.' },

  { id:'tower_5g_mmwave', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'5G NR mmWave Tower (24/28/39 GHz)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:155000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:10, licReq:3,
    desc:'5G NR mmWave gNodeB. n258 (26 GHz), n261 (28 GHz), n260 (39 GHz). 400 MHz channels. 4+ Gbps peak. 32-beam active phased array. 200m coverage radius. Verizon Ultra Wideband. Requires dense deployment — one per city block. Backhaul via fibre or licensed E-band (80 GHz).',
    flavor:'mmWave 5G: 4 Gbps in a 200m bubble. Step outside the bubble: zero signal. Worth it.',
    note:'Verizon deployed mmWave 5G in NFL stadiums and dense urban cores. 4 Gbps possible but 200m range. Rain fade at 28 GHz measurable. Requires fibre to every node. Very expensive per-coverage-km.' },

  { id:'tower_5g_sa_core', brand:'Tower Infrastructure', name:'5G SA Core Network (Standalone)',
    type:'Cellular Tower', cost:200000, rigBonus:5, antennaBonus:10, licReq:3,
    desc:'5G Standalone (SA) core network — 5G Core (5GC) with AMF, SMF, UPF, NRF, PCF, NSSF. Network slicing, edge computing, URLLC ultra-reliable low latency (<1ms). Replaces 4G EPC. Required for full 5G capability. Cloud-native on Kubernetes. AT&T SA 5G core launched 2023.',
    flavor:'5G SA Core live: network slicing active, edge compute deployed, URLLC latency confirmed at 0.8ms.',
    note:'5G NSA uses 4G core — 5G SA replaces it entirely. SA enables true network slicing for private networks. URLLC latency target 1ms enables autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, remote surgery.' },

];

// \u2500\u2500 CIVILIAN SHOP MAIN \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
function civilianShop() {
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2263 HAM RADIO EQUIPMENT SHOP \u2263', 'header');
  printDivider();
  print('  Authorised dealer. All equipment FCC-certified.', 'system');
  print('  No FCC heat. No legal risk. Just good gear.', 'system');
  printBlank();
  print(`  Your XP: ${G.xp}  |  Items owned: ${G.civItems ? G.civItems.length : 0}`, 'amber');
  printBlank();
  print('  Browse by brand:', 'white');
  printBlank();

  const brandChoices = CIVILIAN_BRANDS.map(brand => {
    const items = CIVILIAN_ITEMS.filter(i => i.brand === brand);
    const owned = items.filter(i => G.civItems && G.civItems.includes(i.id)).length;
    return {
      label: `${brand.padEnd(12)}  ${items.length} items${owned ? '  (' + owned + ' owned)' : ''}`,
      fn: () => civilianBrand(brand),
    };
  });
  brandChoices.push({ label: 'Back to shack', fn: mainMenu });
  printChoices(brandChoices);
}

// \u2500\u2500 BRAND LISTING \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
function civilianBrand(brand) {
  if (!G.civItems) G.civItems = [];
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print(`  \u2263 ${brand.toUpperCase()} \u2263`, 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();

  const items = CIVILIAN_ITEMS.filter(i => i.brand === brand);

  items.forEach(it => {
    const owned = G.civItems.includes(it.id);
    const active = G.activeRig === it.name;
    const locked = G.license < (it.licReq || 0);
    const cls = active ? 'success' : owned ? 'cyan' : locked ? 'danger' : 'white';
    const tag = active ? ' \u25c4 ACTIVE' : owned ? ' [owned]' : locked ? ` [need ${LICENSES[it.licReq]}]` : '';
    print(`  ${it.name.padEnd(32)} ${it.cost > 0 ? it.cost + ' XP' : 'FREE'.padEnd(6)}${tag}`, cls);
    print(`    [${it.type}]  ${it.desc}`, 'system');
    printBlank();
  });

  const choices = items.map(it => ({
    label: `${it.name}  \u2014  ${it.cost > 0 ? it.cost + ' XP' : 'FREE'}`,
    fn: () => buyCivilianItem(it, brand),
  }));
  choices.push({ label: 'Back to brand list', fn: civilianShop });
  printChoices(choices);
}

// \u2500\u2500 BUY ITEM \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
function buyCivilianItem(it, brand) {
  if (!G.civItems) G.civItems = [];

  // Already owned \u2014 inspect or set active
  if (G.civItems.includes(it.id)) {
    const isRig = it.rigBonus > 0 && !['Test Equipment','Accessory','Power Supply','Wire Equipment','Intercom Speaker','Paging System','Intranet Terminal'].includes(it.type);
    if (isRig) {
      G.activeRig = it.name;
      G.rigBonus  = it.rigBonus;
      print(`  You power up the ${it.name}.`, 'cyan');
      print(`  ${it.flavor}`, 'amber');
      updateStats();
    } else {
      print(`  You inspect your ${it.name}.`, 'cyan');
      print(`  ${it.note}`, 'system');
    }
    setTimeout(() => civilianBrand(brand), 1000);
    return;
  }

  // License check
  if (G.license < (it.licReq || 0)) {
    print(`  "Sorry \u2014 this rig requires a ${LICENSES[it.licReq]} license or higher."`, 'danger');
    setTimeout(() => civilianBrand(brand), 800);
    return;
  }

  // Free item
  if (it.cost === 0) {
    G.civItems.push(it.id);
    if (!G.inventory.includes(it.name)) G.inventory.push(it.name);
    if (it.antennaBonus) G.antenna = Math.min(10, G.antenna + it.antennaBonus);
    gainXP(it.xpBonus || 0);
    G.morale = Math.min(100, G.morale + (it.moralBonus || 0));
    updateStats();
    print(`  \u2713 Added ${it.name} to your shack.`, 'success');
    print(`  ${it.flavor}`, 'amber');
    setTimeout(() => civilianBrand(brand), 900);
    return;
  }

  // XP check
  if (G.xp < it.cost) {
    print(`  "That\u2019s ${it.cost} XP. You have ${G.xp}. Come back when you\u2019ve made more contacts."`, 'danger');
    setTimeout(() => civilianBrand(brand), 800);
    return;
  }

  // Purchase
  G.xp -= it.cost;
  G.civItems.push(it.id);
  if (!G.inventory.includes(it.name)) G.inventory.push(it.name);

  // Apply effects
  const isRig = it.rigBonus > 0 && !['Test Equipment','Accessory','Power Supply','Wire Equipment','Intercom Speaker','Paging System','Intranet Terminal'].includes(it.type);
  if (isRig) {
    G.activeRig = it.name;
    G.rigBonus  = Math.max(G.rigBonus || 0, it.rigBonus);
    G.radio     = Math.max(G.radio, it.rigBonus);
  }
  if (it.antennaBonus) {
    G.antenna = Math.min(10, G.antenna + it.antennaBonus);
    if (!G.ownedAntennas.includes(it.name)) G.ownedAntennas.push(it.name);
  }
  G.morale = Math.min(100, G.morale + (it.moralBonus || 0));
  gainXP(it.xpBonus || 0);
  updateStats();

  printBlank();
  print(`  \u2554\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2557`, 'success');
  print(`  \u2551  PURCHASED: ${it.name.substring(0,34).padEnd(34)}\u2551`, 'success');
  print(`  \u255a\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u255d`, 'success');
  printBlank();
  print(`  ${it.flavor}`, 'white');
  print(`  ${it.note}`, 'system');
  printBlank();
  if (isRig)          print(`  Rig bonus: +${it.rigBonus}  \u2014  Now your active rig`, 'success');
  if (it.antennaBonus) print(`  Antenna skill +${it.antennaBonus}`, 'success');
  print(`  +${it.xpBonus} XP  |  Morale +${it.moralBonus}`, 'amber');

  setTimeout(() => civilianBrand(brand), 1600);
}


// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550
// \u2500\u2500\u2500 SAVE / LOAD \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550

const SAVE_KEY = 'hamquest_save_v1';

function carDealership(page) {
  page = page || 0;
  const PAGE = 7;
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print("  \u2263 BOB'S HAM RADIO AUTO MALL \u2263", 'header');
  printDivider();
  print('  "A great car makes a great mobile station."', 'amber');
  print('  Costs in XP. Trade knowledge for horsepower.', 'system');
  printBlank();
  const owned = G.ownedVehicles || [];
  if (G.activeVehicle) print('  Driving: ' + G.activeVehicle, 'success');
  print('  Owned: ' + owned.length + '  |  Your XP: ' + G.xp, 'cyan');
  printBlank();
  const pageCars = CARS.slice(page * PAGE, (page + 1) * PAGE);
  const totalPages = Math.ceil(CARS.length / PAGE);
  pageCars.forEach(function(car) {
    const isOwned = owned.includes(car.id);
    const isActive = G.activeVehicle === car.name;
    const canAfford = G.xp >= car.cost;
    const licOk = G.license >= (car.licReq || 0);
    let status = isActive ? ' \u25c4 DRIVING' : isOwned ? ' [owned]' : '';
    if (!licOk) status = ' [needs ' + LICENSES[car.licReq] + ']';
    const cls = isActive ? 'success' : isOwned ? 'cyan' : (canAfford && licOk ? 'white' : 'system');
    print('  ' + car.name.padEnd(30) + ' ' + car.cost + ' XP' + status, cls);
  });
  printBlank();
  const choices = pageCars.map(function(car) {
    return { label: car.name + ' \u2014 ' + car.desc.substring(0, 38), fn: function() { carDetail(car); } };
  });
  if (page > 0) choices.push({ label: '\u2190 Previous', fn: function() { carDealership(page - 1); } });
  if ((page + 1) * PAGE < CARS.length) choices.push({ label: 'Next \u2192', fn: function() { carDealership(page + 1); } });
  choices.push({ label: 'Back to shack', fn: mainMenu });
  printChoices(choices);
}

function carDetail(car) {
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2263 ' + car.name.toUpperCase() + ' \u2263', 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();
  print('  ' + car.desc, 'white');
  printBlank();
  print('  Battery bonus:  +' + car.battBonus + '%', 'amber');
  if (car.propBonus > 0) print('  Propagation:    +' + Math.round(car.propBonus * 100) + '%', 'amber');
  if (car.rigBonus > 0)  print('  Rig bonus:      +' + car.rigBonus, 'amber');
  print('  Morale bonus:   +' + car.moraleBonus, 'amber');
  print('  Cost:           ' + car.cost + ' XP', 'cyan');
  if (car.licReq > 0)    print('  Requires:       ' + LICENSES[car.licReq] + ' license', 'system');
  printBlank();
  print('  Tags: ' + car.tags.join(', '), 'system');
  printBlank();
  const owned = G.ownedVehicles || [];
  const isOwned = owned.includes(car.id);
  const isActive = G.activeVehicle === car.name;
  const licOk = G.license >= (car.licReq || 0);
  const choices = [];
  if (isOwned) {
    if (isActive) {
      print('  You are currently driving this vehicle.', 'success');
      choices.push({ label: 'Park this vehicle', fn: function() {
        _applyVehicleBonus(car, true);
        G.activeVehicle = null;
        updateStats();
        print('  You park the ' + car.name + '.', 'system');
        setTimeout(carDealership, 900);
      }});
    } else {
      choices.push({ label: 'Drive this vehicle', fn: function() { buyOrDriveCar(car); } });
    }
  } else {
    if (!licOk) {
      print('  \u2717 Requires ' + LICENSES[car.licReq] + ' license.', 'danger');
    } else if (G.xp < car.cost) {
      print('  \u2717 Need ' + car.cost + ' XP, have ' + G.xp + '.', 'danger');
    } else {
      choices.push({ label: 'Buy for ' + car.cost + ' XP', fn: function() { buyOrDriveCar(car); } });
    }
  }
  choices.push({ label: 'Back to lot', fn: carDealership });
  printChoices(choices);
}

function _applyVehicleBonus(car, remove) {
  var sign = remove ? -1 : 1;
  G.battery = Math.max(0, Math.min(100, G.battery + sign * car.battBonus));
  G.prop    = Math.max(1, G.prop + sign * (car.propBonus > 0 ? 1 : 0));
  G.radio   = Math.max(1, G.radio + sign * (car.rigBonus || 0));
  updateStats();
}

function buyOrDriveCar(car) {
  if (!G.ownedVehicles) G.ownedVehicles = [];
  var isOwned = G.ownedVehicles.includes(car.id);
  if (!isOwned) {
    if (G.xp < car.cost) {
      print('  \u2717 Not enough XP!', 'danger');
      setTimeout(carDealership, 800);
      return;
    }
    G.xp -= car.cost;
    G.ownedVehicles.push(car.id);
    if (!G.inventory.includes(car.name)) G.inventory.push(car.name);
    printBlank();
    print('  \u2554' + '\u2550'.repeat(46) + '\u2557', 'success');
    print('  \u2551  PURCHASED: ' + car.name.substring(0,34).padEnd(34) + '  \u2551', 'success');
    print('  \u255a' + '\u2550'.repeat(46) + '\u255d', 'success');
    printBlank();
  }
  if (G.activeVehicle) {
    var oldCar = CARS.find(function(c) { return c.name === G.activeVehicle; });
    if (oldCar) _applyVehicleBonus(oldCar, true);
  }
  G.activeVehicle = car.name;
  _applyVehicleBonus(car, false);
  G.morale = Math.min(100, G.morale + car.moraleBonus);
  printBlank();
  print('  ' + car.flavor, 'amber');
  print('  ' + car.note, 'system');
  printBlank();
  print('  Battery: +' + car.battBonus + '%  |  Morale: +' + car.moraleBonus, 'success');
  if (car.propBonus > 0) print('  Propagation bonus applied!', 'success');
  if (car.rigBonus > 0)  print('  Rig quality +' + car.rigBonus, 'success');
  autoSave();
  updateStats();
  setTimeout(mainMenu, 2000);
}


function saveLoadMenu() {
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2263 SAVE / LOAD GAME \u2263', 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();

  const raw = localStorage.getItem(SAVE_KEY);
  if (raw) {
    try {
      const s = JSON.parse(raw);
      const ts = s._savedAt ? new Date(s._savedAt).toLocaleString() : 'Unknown';
      print('  \u2611 Browser save found:', 'success');
      print(`    Call: ${s.callsign || '?'}  |  License: ${(s.licenseNames||['?'])[s.license||0]}`, 'success');
      print(`    QSOs: ${s.qsoCount||0}  |  XP: ${s.xp||0}  |  Saved: ${ts}`, 'success');
    } catch(e) {
      print('  Save file found but unreadable.', 'danger');
    }
  } else {
    print('  No browser save found.', 'system');
  }
  printBlank();

  printChoices([
    { label: '\u2713 Save game (browser)',          fn: saveGame  },
    { label: raw ? '\u2191 Load saved game' : 'Load (no save found)', fn: raw ? loadGame : saveLoadMenu },
    { label: '\u2193 Export save file (.json)',    fn: exportSave },
    { label: '\u2191 Import save file (.json)',    fn: importSave },
    { label: '\u2717 Delete save file',            fn: deleteSave },
    { label: 'Back to shack',                      fn: mainMenu  },
  ]);
}

function exportSave() {
  const saveData = Object.assign({}, G, { _savedAt: new Date().toISOString(), _version: '1.0' });
  delete saveData.choices;
  delete saveData.inputCallback;
  try {
    const json  = JSON.stringify(saveData, null, 2);
    const blob  = new Blob([json], { type: 'application/json' });
    const url   = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
    const ts    = new Date().toISOString().replace(/[:.]/g,'-').slice(0,19);
    const fname = `hamquest-${G.callsign || 'save'}-${ts}.json`;
    const a     = document.createElement('a');
    a.href = url;
    a.download = fname;
    document.body.appendChild(a);
    a.click();
    setTimeout(() => { URL.revokeObjectURL(url); document.body.removeChild(a); }, 1000);
    printBlank();
    print('  \u2713 EXPORT COMPLETE', 'success');
    print('  File: ' + fname, 'amber');
    print('  Save this file to re-import on any device or browser.', 'system');
  } catch(e) {
    print('  \u2717 Export failed: ' + e.message, 'danger');
  }
  setTimeout(saveLoadMenu, 1800);
}

function importSave() {
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2191 IMPORT SAVE FILE', 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();
  print('  Select a .json save file exported from Ham Quest.', 'system');
  print('  Your current progress will be replaced.', 'amber');
  printBlank();

  // Create hidden file input and trigger it
  let fileInput = document.getElementById('save-file-input');
  if (!fileInput) {
    fileInput = document.createElement('input');
    fileInput.type = 'file';
    fileInput.id   = 'save-file-input';
    fileInput.accept = '.json,application/json';
    fileInput.style.display = 'none';
    document.body.appendChild(fileInput);
  }

  fileInput.onchange = function(e) {
    const file = e.target.files[0];
    if (!file) { print('  No file selected.', 'system'); return; }

    const reader = new FileReader();
    reader.onload = function(ev) {
      try {
        const saved = JSON.parse(ev.target.result);
        // Validate it looks like a Ham Quest save
        if (!saved.callsign && !saved.xp) {
          print('  \u2717 File does not appear to be a Ham Quest save.', 'danger');
          setTimeout(saveLoadMenu, 1200);
          return;
        }
        const nonSerialKeys = ['choices','inputCallback','phase','awaitingInput','inputMode'];
        Object.keys(saved).forEach(k => {
          if (!nonSerialKeys.includes(k)) G[k] = saved[k];
        });
        G.phase = 'main';
        G.awaitingInput = false;
        G.inputMode = 'choice';
        G.inputCallback = null;
        G.choices = [];
        // Also store to localStorage so auto-save works
        const resave = Object.assign({}, saved, { _savedAt: new Date().toISOString() });
        delete resave.choices; delete resave.inputCallback;
        localStorage.setItem(SAVE_KEY, JSON.stringify(resave));
        updateStats();
        printBlank();
        print('  \u2713 IMPORT SUCCESSFUL', 'success');
        print(`  Welcome back, ${G.callsign || 'operator'}!`, 'amber');
        print(`  QSOs: ${G.qsoCount||0}  |  XP: ${G.xp||0}  |  License: ${(G.licenseNames||['Novice'])[G.license||0]}`, 'white');
        const savedAt = saved._savedAt ? new Date(saved._savedAt).toLocaleString() : 'Unknown';
        print('  Save dated: ' + savedAt, 'system');
        setTimeout(mainMenu, 2000);
      } catch(err) {
        print('  \u2717 Import failed: ' + err.message, 'danger');
        setTimeout(saveLoadMenu, 1200);
      }
      // Reset file input so same file can be re-selected
      fileInput.value = '';
    };
    reader.readAsText(file);
  };

  fileInput.click();

  // Show cancel option
  printChoices([
    { label: 'Cancel (back to save menu)', fn: saveLoadMenu },
  ]);
}

function saveGame() {
  const saveData = Object.assign({}, G, { _savedAt: new Date().toISOString() });
  // Strip non-serialisable fields
  delete saveData.choices;
  delete saveData.inputCallback;
  try {
    localStorage.setItem(SAVE_KEY, JSON.stringify(saveData));
    printBlank();
    print('  \u2713 GAME SAVED', 'success');
    print(`  ${G.callsign} / ${G.licenseNames[G.license]} / ${G.qsoCount} QSOs / ${G.xp} XP`, 'amber');
    print('  Data stored in browser localStorage.', 'system');
  } catch(e) {
    print('  \u2717 Save failed: ' + e.message, 'danger');
  }
  setTimeout(mainMenu, 1400);
}

function loadGame() {
  const raw = localStorage.getItem(SAVE_KEY);
  if (!raw) { print('  No save file found!', 'danger'); setTimeout(mainMenu, 900); return; }
  try {
    const saved = JSON.parse(raw);
    const nonSerialKeys = ['choices','inputCallback','phase','awaitingInput','inputMode'];
    Object.keys(saved).forEach(k => {
      if (!nonSerialKeys.includes(k)) G[k] = saved[k];
    });
    G.phase = 'main';
    G.awaitingInput = false;
    G.inputMode = 'choice';
    G.inputCallback = null;
    G.choices = [];
    updateStats();
    printBlank();
    print('  \u2713 GAME LOADED', 'success');
    print(`  Welcome back, ${G.callsign}! ${G.qsoCount} QSOs / ${G.xp} XP`, 'amber');
  } catch(e) {
    print('  \u2717 Load failed: ' + e.message, 'danger');
  }
  setTimeout(mainMenu, 1400);
}

function deleteSave() {
  localStorage.removeItem(SAVE_KEY);
  print('  Save file deleted.', 'system');
  setTimeout(saveLoadMenu, 800);
}

// Auto-save on every QSO
function autoSave() {
  try {
    const saveData = Object.assign({}, G, { _savedAt: new Date().toISOString() });
    delete saveData.choices; delete saveData.inputCallback;
    localStorage.setItem(SAVE_KEY, JSON.stringify(saveData));
  } catch(e) { /* silent */ }
}

// Try loading save on startup
(function tryAutoLoad() {
  const raw = localStorage.getItem(SAVE_KEY);
  if (!raw) return;
  try {
    const saved = JSON.parse(raw);
    const nonSerialKeys = ['choices','inputCallback','phase','awaitingInput','inputMode'];
    Object.keys(saved).forEach(k => { if (!nonSerialKeys.includes(k)) G[k] = saved[k]; });
  } catch(e) { /* ignore corrupt save */ }
})();


// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550
// \u2500\u2500\u2500 QSO LOGBOOK \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550

function viewLogbook() {
  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2263 QSO LOGBOOK \u2263', 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();

  const allQSOs = G.qsoLog || [];
  const milEntries = G.milLog || [];
  const satEntries = allQSOs.filter(q => q.type === 'SAT');
  const hfEntries  = allQSOs.filter(q => q.type === 'HF');

  print(`  Total QSOs: ${G.qsoCount}  |  HF: ${hfEntries.length}  |  Satellite: ${satEntries.length}  |  Military intercepts: ${milEntries.length}`, 'amber');
  printBlank();

  printChoices([
    { label: `HF Contacts (${hfEntries.length} logged)`,            fn: () => showLog('HF')  },
    { label: `Satellite QSOs (${satEntries.length} logged)`,         fn: () => showLog('SAT') },
    { label: `Military Signal Log (${milEntries.length} intercepts)`,fn: showMilLog           },
    { label: 'Back to shack', fn: mainMenu },
  ]);
}

function showLog(type, page) {
  page = page || 0;
  const PAGE = 12;
  const entries = (G.qsoLog || []).filter(q => q.type === type);

  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print(`  \u2263 ${type === 'HF' ? 'HF CONTACT LOG' : 'SATELLITE QSO LOG'} \u2263`, 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();

  if (entries.length === 0) {
    print(`  No ${type} contacts logged yet.`, 'system');
    printBlank();
    printChoices([{ label: 'Back to logbook', fn: viewLogbook }]);
    return;
  }

  const start = page * PAGE;
  const slice = entries.slice(start, start + PAGE);

  // Header row
  print('  #    CALLSIGN    LOCATION                      MODE       RST  BAND', 'header');
  print('  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500', 'divider');

  slice.forEach((q, i) => {
    const num = (start + i + 1).toString().padStart(3);
    const call = (q.call || '?').padEnd(12);
    const loc  = (q.loc  || '?').substring(0,30).padEnd(30);
    const mode = (q.mode || '?').substring(0,10).padEnd(10);
    const rpt  = (q.report || '?').padEnd(4);
    const band = (q.band || '?').substring(0,14);
    const cls  = q.dx ? 'cyan' : q.type === 'SAT' ? 'success' : 'white';
    print(`  ${num}  ${call}${loc} ${mode} ${rpt} ${band}`, cls);
  });

  printBlank();
  print(`  Page ${page+1} of ${Math.ceil(entries.length / PAGE)}  |  ${entries.length} total entries`, 'system');
  printBlank();

  const nav = [];
  if (page > 0) nav.push({ label: '\u25c4 Previous page', fn: () => showLog(type, page - 1) });
  if (start + PAGE < entries.length) nav.push({ label: 'Next page \u25ba', fn: () => showLog(type, page + 1) });
  nav.push({ label: 'Back to logbook', fn: viewLogbook });
  printChoices(nav);
}

function showMilLog(page) {
  page = page || 0;
  const PAGE = 10;
  const entries = G.milLog || [];

  printBlank();
  printDivider();
  print('  \u2263 MILITARY SIGNAL INTERCEPT LOG \u2263', 'header');
  printDivider();
  printBlank();

  if (entries.length === 0) {
    print('  No military signals intercepted yet.', 'system');
    print('  They appear rarely while tuning the bands.', 'amber');
    print('  Keep making contacts \u2014 the signals find you.', 'system');
    printBlank();
    printChoices([{ label: 'Back to logbook', fn: viewLogbook }]);
    return;
  }

  const start = page * PAGE;
  const slice = entries.slice(start, start + PAGE);

  print('  SIGNAL NAME                        FREQ         CLASS     DATE', 'header');
  print('  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500', 'divider');

  slice.forEach((m, i) => {
    const num  = (start + i + 1).toString().padStart(3);
    const name = (m.name || '?').substring(0,34).padEnd(34);
    const freq = (m.freq || '?').padEnd(12);
    const cls2 = (m.cls  || '?').padEnd(9);
    const date = m.ts ? new Date(m.ts).toLocaleDateString() : '?';
    const col  = m.cls === 'RADAR' ? 'danger' : m.cls === 'COMMS' ? 'cyan' : m.cls === 'NAV' ? 'amber' : 'system';
    print(`  ${num}  ${name} ${freq} ${cls2} ${date}`, col);
  });

  printBlank();
  print(`  Page ${page+1} of ${Math.ceil(entries.length / PAGE)}  |  ${entries.length} intercepts total`, 'system');
  printBlank();
  print('  LEGEND: RADAR=\u2588 COMMS=\u2588 NAV=\u2588 UNK=\u2588', 'system');
  printBlank();

  const nav = [];
  if (page > 0) nav.push({ label: '\u25c4 Previous', fn: () => showMilLog(page - 1) });
  if (start + PAGE < entries.length) nav.push({ label: 'Next \u25ba', fn: () => showMilLog(page + 1) });
  nav.push({ label: 'Back to logbook', fn: viewLogbook });
  printChoices(nav);
}


// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550
// \u2500\u2500\u2500 MILITARY SIGNAL DATABASE \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500
// \u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550

const MIL_SIGNALS = [
  // OVER-THE-HORIZON RADAR
  { name:"Ghadir OTH Radar",               freq:"8-30 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"Iran", desc:"Iranian over-the-horizon radar. Distinctive chirped pulse sweeps across HF. Range: 3000 km.",
    sound:"A periodic sweeping chirp, approximately 50 ms pulses, 25 Hz repetition rate.",
    detail:"Operated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy. Detects surface vessels and low-altitude aircraft." },
  { name:"OTH-SW OTH Radar",               freq:"5-28 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Russian Over-the-Horizon Surface Wave radar. Monitors ship movements in adjacent seas.",
    sound:"Repetitive broadband chirp sweeps, similar to Duga but shorter pulse duration.",
    detail:"Surface-wave propagation means it can monitor sea lanes far beyond line-of-sight." },
  { name:"29B6 Kontayner OTH Radar",        freq:"3-30 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Russian 29B6 Kontayner backscatter OTH radar, covering Europe and Asia.",
    sound:"Very strong broadband interference \u2014 destroys entire ham bands when operating. Pulse rate ~10 Hz.",
    detail:"Deployed at Kovylkino and Zelenaya Gora. Replacement for the legendary Duga radar system." },
  { name:"77Ya6 Voronezh Radar",            freq:"Decametric HF",    cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Voronezh-M/DM early warning radar. Modular phased-array OTH ballistic missile detection.",
    sound:"Wideband pulsed interference across lower HF bands during active operations.",
    detail:"Provides missile launch detection coverage for all Russian border directions." },
  { name:"Duga OTH Radar (Ghost)",          freq:"7-19 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.15,
    nation:"Russia/Soviet", desc:"The Duga (NATO: Steel Yard, hams: Russian Woodpecker). Cold War OTH radar echo.",
    sound:"The unmistakable woodpecker tap: 10 pulses/sec, repetitive broadband interference.",
    detail:"Original Duga decommissioned 1989. This ghost signal is a modern Duga-class successor." },
  { name:"Chinese Foghorn OTH Radar",       freq:"13-23 MHz",        cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"China", desc:"Chinese over-the-horizon radar with distinctive foghorn-like audio signature.",
    sound:"Deep rhythmic pulse, 50 Hz repetition, broad bandwidth. Sounds exactly like a foghorn.",
    detail:"Monitoring maritime activity and aircraft in the South China Sea and Pacific." },
  { name:"PLUTO II OTH Radar",              freq:"6-20 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"France", desc:"French OTH radar system. Atlantic and Mediterranean maritime surveillance.",
    sound:"Regular chirp sweeps at moderate power. Less disruptive than Russian OTH systems.",
    detail:"Joint French Navy/Air Force operation. Part of European coastal defense network." },
  { name:"Jindalee (JORN)",                 freq:"5-30 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Australia", desc:"Jindalee Operational Radar Network. Australian OTH radar covering northern approaches.",
    sound:"Wideband chirp sweeps visible across 1-3 MHz chunks of the HF spectrum.",
    detail:"Three JORN sites: Longreach QLD, Laverton WA, Holt NT. Covers 37,000 km of approaches." },
  { name:"ROTHR (Relocatable OTH Radar)",   freq:"5-28 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"USA", desc:"US Navy Relocatable Over-The-Horizon Radar. Counter-narcotics surveillance in Caribbean.",
    sound:"Characteristic broadband chirp, 25 ms pulse, 50 Hz PRF. Amateur bands affected.",
    detail:"ROTHR sites in Virginia, Texas, and Puerto Rico monitoring drug trafficking routes." },
  { name:"Cobra Dane Radar",                freq:"1215-1400 MHz",    cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.15,
    nation:"USA", desc:"USAF phased-array radar on Shemya Island, Alaska. Space object tracking and ICBM monitoring.",
    sound:"L-band pulsed radar emissions detectable via wideband receiver at long range.",
    detail:"15,000 elements. Tracks Soviet/Russian missile tests and space debris since 1976." },
  { name:"Podsolnukh Sunflower Radar",      freq:"3-30 MHz SW",      cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Russian Podsolnukh surface-wave OTH radar. 450 km range for ship detection.",
    sound:"Surface-wave radar emissions: wideband swept pulses at low HF frequencies.",
    detail:"Deployed around Russian coastlines. Monitors Caspian, Black Sea, and Arctic shipping." },
  { name:"Unknown FMCW Radar (33p36)",      freq:"26.8 MHz",         cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.12,
    nation:"Unknown", desc:"Unidentified narrow FMCW radar signal. Appears intermittently on 26.8 MHz.",
    sound:"Continuous frequency-modulated carrier sweep. Very narrow bandwidth, ~30 Hz sweep.",
    detail:"Signal characteristics suggest over-the-horizon surface wave radar, nation unknown." },
  { name:"Unknown Radar (Toronto vicinity)",freq:"412-430 MHz",      cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.1,
    nation:"Unknown/Canada", desc:"Unidentified radar emissions detected near Toronto, Canada. Strong, intermittent.",
    sound:"Pulsed UHF radar emissions. Short pulse width, high PRF. Military or aviation origin.",
    detail:"Detected by Canadian amateur radio operators. Source never officially acknowledged." },
  { name:"GRAVES Space Surveillance Radar", freq:"143.050 MHz",      cls:"RADAR", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"France", desc:"French Space Surveillance System. Monitors orbital objects \u2014 you can hear echoes off satellites.",
    sound:"Continuous wave carrier at 143.050 MHz. Doppler echoes audible when satellite passes.",
    detail:"Amateurs use GRAVES echoes to spot meteors and satellites. Entirely passive receive." },

  // COMMUNICATIONS SYSTEMS
  { name:"HFGCS (High Freq Global Comms)",  freq:"4724/6739/8992/11175/13200/15016 MHz", cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.5,
    nation:"USA", desc:"USAF High Frequency Global Communications System. Global command net for nuclear command.",
    sound:"USB voice and ALE data. EAM broadcasts: 'SKYKING SKYKING DO NOT ANSWER' followed by code groups.",
    detail:"Broadcasts Emergency Action Messages 24/7. The SKYKINGs are the nuclear command heartbeat of the USAF." },
  { name:"The Buzzer (UVB-76 / ZhUOZ)",     freq:"4625 kHz",         cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.45,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"The famous Buzzer. A 24/7 buzzing tone occasionally interrupted by voice callouts.",
    sound:"Repetitive buzz, ~25 per minute. Rare voice: ZHENYA, ANNA, MIKHAIL... then number groups.",
    detail:"Operating since the 1970s. Purpose officially unknown. Likely a military dead man switch or coordination channel." },
  { name:"The Pip",                         freq:"5448 kHz",         cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.4,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Another Russian enigma station. Regular single-tone pips interrupted by voice messages.",
    sound:"Single sharp pip, approximately 1 per 2 seconds. Occasionally silent for voice transmissions.",
    detail:"Part of the same Russian military communication network as the Buzzer and Squeaky Wheel." },
  { name:"The Alarm",                       freq:"3756 kHz",         cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Russian station transmitting an alarm-like tone. Suspected military coordination signal.",
    sound:"Alternating two-tone alarm sound. Continuous 24/7 broadcast.",
    detail:"Exact purpose classified. Co-located and operationally linked with the Buzzer network." },
  { name:"The Goose",                       freq:"4307 kHz",         cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Russian mystery signal with goose-like honking audio. Military frequency coordination.",
    sound:"Rhythmic honking tones. Steady cadence. Unmistakable once you have heard it.",
    detail:"Part of the family of Russian HF enigma beacons monitoring channel availability." },
  { name:"One Beep / Two Beeps",            freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.35,
    nation:"Russia", desc:"Pair of Russian beacon stations: one emitting single beeps, one emitting double beeps.",
    sound:"Single or double tone pulses at regular intervals. Directional beacon or dead-man signal.",

  { name:"F07 Number Station",              freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,

    sound:"Female voice, French language, structured 5-digit number groups. End of message: 0000.",

  { name:"STANAG 4285",                     freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.4,

    sound:"Characteristic PSK tones \u2014 sounds like a waterfall of parallel lines on SDR spectrum display.",

  { name:"STANAG 4481 FSK",                 freq:"VLF/LF",           cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"NATO", desc:"NATO VLF/LF FSK standard for submarine communications. Very slow data rate.",

  { name:"STANAG 4415",                     freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.35,

    sound:"BPSK tone bursts. Narrow bandwidth, robust to fading. Recognisable turquoise stripe on waterfall.",
    detail:"Optimised for reliability over raw throughput. Used when channel conditions are challenging." },
  { name:"STANAG 4539",                     freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"NATO", desc:"NATO high-throughput HF data standard. Wideband OFDM for broadband HF links.",
    sound:"Wideband OFDM signal \u2014 multiple carriers visible on SDR waterfall spanning several kHz.",

  { name:"STANAG 4197",                     freq:"Various VHF",      cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"NATO", desc:"NATO voice scrambler standard used in military aircraft communications.",
    sound:"Scrambled voice that sounds like reversed or pitch-shifted speech. Unintelligible without key.",
    detail:"Analogue voice inversion / frequency hopping variant used in legacy aircraft systems." },
  { name:"STANAG 5065 Link-11",             freq:"2-30 MHz HF / UHF",cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"NATO", desc:"NATO Link-11 tactical data link. Ships and aircraft share tactical picture in real time.",
    sound:"16-tone FSK signal. Characteristic warbling sound instantly recognisable to naval operators.",
    detail:"Replaced by Link-22 in newer ships, but Link-11 still in service worldwide." },
  { name:"Link-11 UHF Variant",             freq:"225-400 MHz UHF",  cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"NATO", desc:"Link-11 variant operating in the military UHF band for shorter-range tactical links.",
    sound:"Same 16-tone DTDMA waveform as HF Link-11 but on UHF carrier.",
    detail:"Used between ships in a task group where HF propagation is unreliable at close range." },
  { name:"MIL-STD-188-110 Serial",         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.4,
    nation:"USA/NATO", desc:"US/NATO standard serial HF data modem. 75-2400 bps single-tone PSK/QAM.",
    sound:"Continuous PSK tone at fixed frequency. Sounds like a dial-up modem operating on shortwave.",

  { name:"MIL-STD-188-110 App-A 16-Tone", freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,

    sound:"16 parallel tones spanning 3 kHz. Dense parallel lines on the SDR waterfall display.",

  { name:"MIL-STD-188-110 App-B 39-Tone", freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"USA/NATO", desc:"39-tone OFDM variant for broadband military HF communications.",
    sound:"Wide OFDM comb across 9 kHz bandwidth. Very distinctive dense waterfall signature.",
    detail:"Supports rates up to 9600 bps under good channel conditions." },
  { name:"ALE 2G (MIL-STD-188-141A)",      freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.5,

    sound:"Brief AMD sounding sequence: characteristic 8-tone sweep then handshake tones.",

  { name:"ALE 3G ARCS",                    freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,

  { name:"HFDVL (HF Data and Voice Link)",  freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"Australia", desc:"Australian Defence Force HF Data and Voice Link. STANAG-compatible ADF system.",
    sound:"ADF-specific framing around standard STANAG waveforms. Australian regional HF net.",

  { name:"MHFCS (Modernised HF Comms)",    freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,

  { name:"Thales Systeme 3000 Skyhopper",  freq:"VHF/UHF",          cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.15,

    sound:"Rapid frequency hops \u2014 sounds like brief chirps scattered across a wide band.",
    detail:"Installed in Mirage, Rafale, and Tiger helicopter platforms." },

    sound:"Wideband OFDM comb. Similar to STANAG 4539 but with Russian framing structure.",
    detail:"Part of the Russian digital HF modernisation programme post-2010." },
  { name:"CIS-12",                         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.35,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 12-tone OFDM signal. One of the most commonly heard Russian military HF modes.",
    sound:"12 evenly spaced tones, 250 Hz spacing. Very clean parallel lines on waterfall.",
    detail:"Widely used across Russian armed forces for digital data. Heard daily on 10-20 MHz." },
  { name:"CIS-16",                         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.3,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 16-tone variant. Slight variation on CIS-12 structure.",
    sound:"16 parallel tones. Slightly wider than CIS-12. Characteristic Russian military spacing.",
    detail:"Used in parallel with CIS-12 depending on channel bandwidth available." },
  { name:"CIS-14",                         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.28,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 14-tone parallel FSK. Part of the CIS family.",
    sound:"14 parallel tones, steady FSK modulation. Waterfall shows 14 distinct lines.",
    detail:"Less common than CIS-12/16 but regularly observed on Russian military channels." },
  { name:"CIS-20",                         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 20-tone military HF parallel modem.",
    sound:"20 parallel tones spanning approximately 3 kHz. Dense comb pattern.",
    detail:"Used for higher-bandwidth data when channel conditions permit." },
  { name:"CIS-48",                         freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 48-tone wideband OFDM modem. High-capacity military link.",
    sound:"Very dense 48-tone comb across wide bandwidth. Impressive waterfall display.",
    detail:"High-capacity version of the CIS parallel tone family. Naval and strategic comms." },
  { name:"CIS-112",                        freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.15,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 112-tone OFDM. Very wideband HF modem for high-data-rate links.",
    sound:"Extraordinarily wide comb of 112 tones. Spans entire amateur HF segments when active.",
    detail:"One of the widest bandwidth Russian HF signals. Rarely observed at full power." },
  { name:"CIS-128",                        freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.12,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 128-tone variant. Maximum bandwidth CIS family modem.",
    sound:"128 tones across approximately 25 kHz. Essentially blots out entire amateur allocations.",
    detail:"The widest known CIS modem. Observed primarily on frequencies above 10 MHz." },
  { name:"CIS-1280",                       freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.1,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian high-symbol-rate 1280-baud data link.",
    sound:"Very rapid symbol rate creates distinctive rushing sound on USB demodulation.",
    detail:"High symbol rate suits time-critical tactical data transfer requirements." },
  { name:"CIS-300",                        freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 300-baud FSK modem. Older format still in regular use.",
    sound:"Classic FSK tones at 300 baud. Sounds like early dial-up modem on shortwave.",
    detail:"Resilient at low data rates. Keeps working when modern wideband modes fail." },
  { name:"CIS-3000",                       freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.15,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 3000-baud high-speed HF data link.",
    sound:"Rapid high-density FSK. Sounds like a fast dial-up modem running at maximum rate.",
    detail:"3000 baud is fast for HF. Requires excellent channel conditions." },
  { name:"CIS-36-50",                      freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 36-tone 50-baud VFT composite. Frequency-division multiplexed channels.",
    sound:"36 narrowband channels, each running 50 baud. Very distinctive dense array.",
    detail:"Multiplexes 36 teleprinter channels onto a single HF carrier. High efficiency." },
  { name:"CIS-40.5",                       freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.18,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 40.5 kHz bandwidth OFDM signal. Unusually precise bandwidth signature.",
    sound:"Wide OFDM with sharp spectral edges at exactly 40.5 kHz. Unique fingerprint.",
    detail:"The precise bandwidth is a consistent identification feature." },
  { name:"CIS-50-50",                      freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.2,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 50-tone 50-baud multiplexed link.",
    sound:"50 narrow tones at 50 baud each. Maximum multiplexing density in the CIS family.",
    detail:"Packs 50 independent teleprinter channels into a single HF transmission." },
  { name:"CIS-5275",                       freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.12,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian high-speed burst modem. 5275 bps peak data rate.",
    sound:"Rapid burst transmissions with silent intervals. High-speed data with channel probing.",
    detail:"Burst mode minimises exposure time. Used for intelligence data exfiltration via HF." },
  { name:"CIS-8181",                       freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.1,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Russian 8181-baud OFDM variant. Among the highest-rate CIS signals.",
    sound:"Dense broadband OFDM, very rapid symbol transitions. Hard to demodulate.",
    detail:"8181 baud approaches the theoretical maximum for standard HF channels." },
  { name:"CIS 3x100 VFT",                 freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.25,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Three-channel 100 Hz voice-frequency telegraphy multiplex.",
    sound:"Three parallel narrowband channels. Each carries independent 100 Hz FSK stream.",
    detail:"Voice frequency telegraphy: analogue technique using audio tones over SSB carrier." },
  { name:"CIS 3x144 VFT",                 freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.22,
    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Three-channel 144 Hz spacing VFT. Slightly wider channels than 3x100.",
    sound:"Three slightly broader FSK channels. Familiar CIS VFT structure with wider tone spacing.",
    detail:"144 Hz spacing gives better selectivity between adjacent channels." },

    nation:"Russia/CIS", desc:"Three Baudot/ITA-2 teleprinter channels multiplexed on VFT subcarriers.",
    sound:"Three Baudot streams audible as interlocking FSK tones. Characteristic CIS texture.",
    detail:"Baudot teleprinter encoding predates ASCII. Still used by Russian military for reliability." },
  { name:"CIS FTM-4",                     freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.18,

    sound:"16-tone FSK with rapid tone transitions. Waterfall shows diagonal striping pattern.",

  { name:"CIS MFSK-21-13",               freq:"Various HF",       cls:"COMMS", rarity:0.18,

    sound:"1200 baud PSK with cryptographic scrambling. Sounds like a fast modem with random structure.",

    sound:"Powerful MSK at 19.8 kHz. Detectable worldwide on VLF receiver.",

    sound:"VLF MSK at 16.0 kHz. NATO submarine communications, North Atlantic.",
